insight: Centurion was considered overtuned in mid-2018: expert masn6811 argued that almost every set with Centurion becomes a Centurion set, even when counters like Brooder are present, and that you need very strong reasons to avoid buying it. A cost increase to at least 19 gold was suggested as a starting nerf.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about overtuned units and balance suggestions for Centurion in mid-2018.

insight: Making Centurion require triple blue (CBBBA) was considered a bad balancing approach because Centurion sets already feel homogeneous with everyone on CBBA; requiring CBBBA would further reduce decision space, whereas CBBA at least preserves a decision about a third Blastforge when Omega Splitter is in the set.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about how to nerf Centurion without homogenizing the tech path further.

insight: Drake's historical cost progression illustrates the difficulty of balancing large blue attackers: at 15BBB it was heavily P2-favored, at 16BBB it was decently P1-favored, and at 17BBB it became nearly unplayable — showing that even small gold cost changes can drastically swing player advantage for high-cost units.
author: masn6811
context: Historical balance discussion comparing Drake's successive nerfs and their effects on player advantage.

insight: The old Centrifuge at 12EEE cost returning 20 gold was considered balanced in context because other powerful units (e.g., 15BBB Drake) competed with it, but after the competitive landscape changed it became obviously too strong. A 9-for-12 gold version was also judged as overpowered, and 6-for-6 was seen as an acceptable middle-ground buff.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about the history of Centrifuge balance iterations and what an appropriate rework might look like.

insight: Cauterizer is notable not just as preemptive soak but because it generates RRRR each turn, enabling consistent Husk purchases; masn6811 argued this sustained red production and its threat-handling value make it stronger than its clunky tech cost suggests.
author: masn6811
context: Debate about whether Cauterizer is worth its cost compared to Wall plus Engineers as preemptive soak.

insight: Cauterizer functions similarly to a vigilant blocker in that buying it before an opponent's threat resolves (e.g., an Odin) neutralizes that threat for a turn, giving the buyer time to establish their own offense; one player reported winning a second-player Odin game by buying Cauterizer first to absorb the initial Odin hit.
author: swapgo
context: Discussion on Cauterizer's role as preemptive soak against imminent pressure units.

insight: Antima Comet is described as Prismata's closest equivalent to an X-cost spell, but unlike a true X-spell it still allows partial use of resources; a notable interaction is using leftover gold from an Antima turn to buy Cauterizer, which costs less than 6 Engineers and also deals 6 damage, making it a resource-efficient combo.
author: swapgo
context: Discussion about X-cost mechanics in Prismata and the flexibility Antima Comet offers compared to sinking all gold.

insight: Kinetic Driver (referred to as 'kinetic') is considered by jeacaveo and junkmail to be one of the stronger units in the game, with its key value being the threat of sniping opponent tech buildings (especially Blastforges) or high-value units. In low-economy games where the opponent is soaking with Walls, Kinetic Driver becomes particularly punishing.
author: junkmail
context: Community debate on which units are weakest, during which junkmail and jeacaveo defended Kinetic Driver as strong due to its snipe threat.

insight: Pixie is considered the weakest or among the weakest advanced units, being the most resource-inefficient unit in the game unless blue production costs nothing. Players still buy it when forced into a triple-blue tech path as a blue sink, but it is rarely worth buying otherwise.
author: consensus
context: Community discussion on worst units in the game, with multiple players (namington, junkmail, jeacaveo, totally_not_fbi) converging on Pixie as the weakest.

insight: Auride Core is a solid early-game blue sink when you would otherwise be making Drones, providing reliable value; its usefulness decreases once you already have established damage. It is not considered among the worst units, and buying one or two rarely loses the game, though it is weaker than Thermite as an absorb-denial tool.
author: namington
context: Discussion on weakest units where Auride Core was nominated and then defended by namington and junkmail.

insight: A hyperaggressive Militia rush (DD/DB opening) is normally expected to hard-counter big red (double Animus) strategies, but an old version of Corpus combined with Tatsu Nullifier was so overpowered that DD/DD/DAA still beat DD/DB plus Militia — indicating the combination was fundamentally broken rather than a skill or timing issue.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 recounting a ladder game where Militia rush failed to beat an old Corpus + Tatsu opening, used as evidence that old Corpus was overtuned.

insight: The term 'inflation' as used in Prismata refers not to rising prices (which don't exist in Prismata) but to the diminishing marginal value of gold over time — early gold is more valuable because purchases made sooner begin generating returns sooner. 'Rate of return on investment' was proposed as a more precise term.
author: namington
context: namington clarifying the technical meaning of 'inflation' as used in Prismata strategy discussions.

insight: If a player builds a Conduit on turn 3, the natural timing to fire Zemora Voidbringer is around turn 12, when accumulated green production allows a well-timed shot.
author: namington
context: Brief aside about Zemora Voidbringer timing relative to a turn-3 Conduit purchase.
